ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LQ covers sparkling wine broadly; no Champagne-specific sub-code available in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match applied.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard champagne flute holds approximately 120 ml (120 g) of Champagne, which is the typical single serving at a social occasion.

nutrient_source.title: Alcoholic beverage, wine, table, white — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173190); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for Champagne (D01050001)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore reports energy 84 kcal, carbohydrate 21 g, sodium 10 mg per 100 g for Champagne. USDA SR Legacy FDC 173190 (white table wine) reports energy 82 kcal, protein 0.07 g, fat 0 g, carbohydrate 2.6 g per 100 g. The HPB carbohydrate value of 21 g/100 g is unusually high for a dry sparkling wine (typical range 1–5 g/100 g) and likely reflects a demi-sec or sweet style or a data entry anomaly; however, as HPB is the canonical primary source for this Singapore record, the HPB values are preserved as-is and the discrepancy is documented in notes. Protein and fat are confirmed zero per both sources. Saturated fat confirmed 0 g. Sugar per USDA SR Legacy for white wine: 1.4 g/100 g — used as best available approved-source estimate since HPB reports 0 g sugar which is inconsistent with the 21 g carbohydrate figure; HPB sugar value of 0 g retained with caveat noted.

gi_source.title: Alcoholic beverages and glycemic index — analogous estimate based on white wine GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Champagne exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Champagne is a sparkling white wine; GI is estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on white wine analogues reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 25.2) / 100 ≈ 11.3 for a 120 g serving (21 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.2 = 25.2 g carbohydrate per serving). Note: the HPB carbohydrate value of 21 g/100 g is atypically high for a dry champagne; if actual carbohydrate is closer to 3 g/100 g (USDA), GL per serving would be approximately 1.6, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content (approximately 11–12% ABV) directly raises blood pressure and reduces antihypertensive medication efficacy
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Champagne and all alcoholic beverages, as even moderate alcohol intake acutely elevates systolic and diastolic blood pressure and antagonises antihypertensive drug action.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis causing hypoglycaemia, followed by rebound hyperglycaemia; interacts with insulin and sulfonylureas
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Champagne; if consumed socially, limit to one small glass with food, never on an empty stomach, and monitor blood glucose closely due to unpredictable glycaemic fluctuations and medication interactions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol raises triglycerides, contributes to arrhythmia risk (holiday heart syndrome), and interacts with anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Champagne consumption, as alcohol raises triglycerides, can trigger atrial fibrillation, and interacts with anticoagulants such as warfarin and antiplatelet agents.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Alcohol is pro-inflammatory and critically contraindicated with methotrexate (hepatotoxicity) and biologic DMARDs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Champagne entirely, as alcohol is pro-inflammatory, can trigger disease flares, and is contraindicated with methotrexate due to severe hepatotoxicity risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Champagne is a prestigious French sparkling wine produced exclusively in the Champagne region of northeastern France using the traditional méthode champenoise (traditional method). It is made primarily from Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier grapes, which undergo a secondary fermentation inside the bottle to produce its characteristic fine bubbles and effervescence. Champagne ranges in style from Brut Nature (bone dry) to Doux (very sweet), with Brut being the most common style served globally. It has a pale golden colour, a crisp, yeasty, and toasty flavour profile with notes of green apple, citrus, brioche, and almond, and a persistent mousse (bubble stream). Nutritionally, Champagne provides approximately 84 kcal per 100 ml (per HPB Singapore data), with negligible protein and fat. The carbohydrate content varies significantly by style — dry Brut champagnes typically contain 1–3 g per 100 ml, while sweeter styles may contain up to 5 g or more. Champagne contains alcohol (approximately 11–12% ABV), which is the primary clinical concern. Alcohol is contraindicated or requires strict caution in individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, rheumatoid arthritis, liver disease, and pregnancy. Champagne is not halal and is not suitable for individuals who abstain from alcohol for religious, medical, or personal reasons. It is commonly consumed at celebrations, social events, and fine dining occasions. A standard serving is one champagne flute (approximately 120 ml). Champagne contains sulphites as a natural by-product of fermentation and as a preservative, which may trigger reactions in sulphite-sensitive individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a glass of champagne?
Can I drink champagne if I have high blood pressure?
What is the alcohol content of champagne?
Is champagne suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients?

Pinot Noir: red grape variety used in champagne blending
Pinot Meunier: red grape variety used in champagne blending

confidence_reason: The HPB Singapore database is an approved primary source and is used as the canonical nutrient anchor for this record. However, the HPB carbohydrate value of 21 g/100 g is atypically high for a dry sparkling wine (USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 2.6 g/100 g for white wine), suggesting the HPB figure may reflect a sweet champagne style or a data entry issue. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate from white wine data. Potassium, calcium, iron, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and water values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 173190 as supplementary approved-source data.
notes: ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: protein_g is confirmed zero — Champagne is a fermented grape beverage containing no protein by product composition, confirmed by both HPB Singapore and USDA SR Legacy FDC 173190. fat_g is confirmed zero — Champagne contains no fat by product composition, confirmed by both HPB Singapore and USDA SR Legacy. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero for the same reason. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 because detailed B-vitamin and selenium data for Champagne specifically are not reported in the HPB Singapore database, and USDA SR Legacy FDC 173190 (white table wine) does not provide reliable values for these micronutrients for sparkling wine as of 2026. CARBOHYDRATE ANOMALY: The HPB Singapore value of 21 g carbohydrate per 100 g is preserved as the canonical primary source value but is flagged as atypically high for a dry Brut champagne. USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 2.6 g/100 g for white table wine. The discrepancy may reflect a sweet champagne style (Doux or Demi-Sec) or a data entry issue in the HPB record. Downstream users should verify the carbohydrate value against the specific champagne style. SUGAR ANOMALY: HPB reports 0 g sugar despite 21 g carbohydrate, which is internally inconsistent; this is preserved as reported but flagged. GI NOTE: No direct GI measurement for Champagne exists; GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on white wine data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). If actual carbohydrate is closer to USDA values (~2.6 g/100 g), the glycemic load per serving would be negligible (~1.6). DIETARY FLAGS: is_vegan set to false because many champagnes use animal-derived fining agents (isinglass, casein, egg albumin); individual brands vary. SULPHITES: Champagne contains sulphites (naturally occurring and added as preservative); relevant for sulphite-sensitive individuals.
